coredata.dc:title: Experimental exploration of rheological behavior of polyethylene glycol-carbon dot nanofluid: Introducing a robust artificial intelligence paradigm optimized with unscented Kalman filter technique
coredata.dc:creator: Shahsavar, Amin
Amin Mirzaei, Mohamad
Shaham, Aidin
Jamei, Mehdi
Karbasi, Masoud
Seifikar, Fatemeh
Azizian, Saeid
coredata.dc:description:
In the present study, the polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG200)-based nanofluid containing carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles was synthesized, and its rheological behavior at different temperatures and nanoparticle concentrations (
φ
) was investigated. The values considered for
φ
were 0%, 1% and 3% and 7% the values considered for temperature were 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C. It was observed that the PEG200 has a Newtonian behavior, and the nanofluid has a non-Newtonian behavior which is amplified with increasing temperature. Also, a decreasing and increasing trend of viscosity was observed with temperature and
φ
. As another novelty of this research, a robust novel artificial neural network (ANN) model integrated with an unscented Kalman filter (UKF-ANN) was presented for accurate estimation of the viscosity of the PEG-CD nanofluid based on
φ
, temperature, and shear rate as the input features. Besides, two efficient data-driven approaches, including classical perceptron ANN (MLP) and response surface methodology (RSM) were developed to examine and evaluate the robustness of UKF-ANN model. The statistical and infographic assessment indicated that the UKF-ANN outperformed the MLP and RSM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot nanofluid
Polyethylene glycol
Viscosity
Unscented Kalman filter
Artificial neural network
Response surface methodology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The steps of the carbonaceous nanofluid synthesis.
(a) The TEM image of the carbon particles in the prepared nanofluids and (b) the corresponding particle size distribution histogram.
DLS histogram of the CDs nanofluid, 10 times diluted with polyethylene glycol.
FT-IR spectra of the pure polyethylene glycol and the prepared CDs nanofluids.
Structure of an ANN trained by UKF algorithm (UKF-ANN).

Rheological behaviour of PEG-CD nanofluid versus temperature for
φ
= 0%.

Rheological behaviour of PEG-CD nanofluid versus temperature for
φ
= 1%.

Rheological behaviour of PEG-CD nanofluid versus temperature for
φ
= 3%.

Rheological behaviour of PEG-CD nanofluid versus temperature for
φ
= 7%.
Correlogram of the datasets used in the prediction of the dynamic viscosity.
Flowchart of the viscosity estimating process using three data-driven models for the PEG-carbon dot nanofluid.
Scatter plots to compare the predictive performance of each model versus the experimental datasets in both training and testing phases.
Rain cloud probability distribution function plot of the experimental and each model based on the smooth kernel for all the data points used in the simulation.
The relative error in 3D contour (Left) and violin plot (Right) of all predictive models to estimate the viscosity of nanofluid.
Statistical properties of the experimental datasets.
Statistical analysis of RSM approach to obtain a quadratic relationship.
Goodness-of-fit related to the data-driven models to predict the viscosity of PEG-carbon dot nanofluid.

head.ce:title: Experimental exploration of rheological behavior of polyethylene glycol-carbon dot nanofluid: Introducing a robust artificial intelligence paradigm optimized with unscented Kalman filter technique

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Kermanshah University of Technology, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Kermanshah University of Technology, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Shohadaye Hoveizeh Campus of Technology, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Dashte Azadegan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Engineering, Shohadaye Hoveizeh Campus of Technology, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Dashte Azadegan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Water Engineering Department, Faculty of Agriculture, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Water Engineering Department, Faculty of Agriculture, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In the present study, the polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG200)-based nanofluid containing carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles was synthesized, and its rheological behavior at different temperatures and nanoparticle concentrations (
φ
) was investigated. The values considered for
φ
were 0%, 1% and 3% and 7% the values considered for temperature were 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C. It was observed that the PEG200 has a Newtonian behavior, and the nanofluid has a non-Newtonian behavior which is amplified with increasing temperature. Also, a decreasing and increasing trend of viscosity was observed with temperature and
φ
. As another novelty of this research, a robust novel artificial neural network (ANN) model integrated with an unscented Kalman filter (UKF-ANN) was presented for accurate estimation of the viscosity of the PEG-CD nanofluid based on
φ
, temperature, and shear rate as the input features. Besides, two efficient data-driven approaches, including classical perceptron ANN (MLP) and response surface methodology (RSM) were developed to examine and evaluate the robustness of UKF-ANN model. The statistical and infographic assessment indicated that the UKF-ANN outperformed the MLP and RSM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: The idea and concept of nanotechnology were established on December 29, 1959, with a lecture by physicist Richard Feynman at the American Physical Society Symposium at the California Institute of Technology [1]. He addressed the concept of nanotechnology in a lecture entitled “Many Areas Still Have to Be Discovered in Physics,” but it was only years later that the term nanotechnology was first used by scientific societies. In his speech, Feynman described a process by which scientists could manipulate and control individual atoms and molecules. About ten years later, Professor Norio Taniguchi coined the term nanotechnology while researching ultra-precision machines [2]. Today, even in the lives of ordinary people, the name of nanotechnology is often heard and seen as well as various products that are made or improved with the help of this technology.

Nanofluids are one of the nanotechnology products that have been widely welcomed by researchers seeking to improve the performance of different heat transfer systems [3]. These fluids, which are modern coolants, include a base fluid and nanoparticles [4]. Water, ethylene glycol, and oil are the base fluids used in nanofluids, while the nanoparticles used in nanofluids are mainly metals (such as Cu, Al, and Fe), metal oxides (such as CuO, Al2O3, SiO2) or carbon nanotubes [5].

After studies showed that nanofluids have better thermal performance than conventional coolants, researchers from around the world conducted extensive research on the synthesis of new nanofluids [6-8], measuring their thermophysical properties [9-11], and investigating their cooling performance in various applications such as batteries [12-14], CPUs [15-17], heat exchangers [18-20], solar systems [21-23], cavities [24-26] and engines [27-29] are done. Also, many researchers have tried to combine nanofluids with other methods of improving heat transfer, such as fin [30-32], turbulator [33-35], magnetic field [36-38], and porous media [39-41].

The viscosity of nanofluids has a significant effect on their cooling performance, and for this reason, many studies have been performed on measuring the viscosity of nanofluids [42]. Esfe et al. [43] inspected the rheological features of 5 W50 engine oil-MWCNT/Al2O3 nanofluid in terms of nanoparticle concentration, temperature, and shear rate. Their results showed the non-Newtonian behavior of nanofluids at low shear rates. Ajith et al. [44] used two stainless-steel and nylon spindles to measure the viscosity of magnesium ferrite nanofluid in the presence of a magnetic field to investigate the effect of spindle material on the results. The results revealed that the use of a nylon spindle significantly reduces the error in measuring viscosity. Zhu [45] experimentally analyzed the viscosity of water/ethylene glycol-MWCNT/WO3 nanofluid. They studied the effect of temperature and concentration on viscosity and found that increasing temperature and concentration leads to decreasing and increasing viscosity, respectively. Colak et al. [46] measured the viscosity of water-zirconium oxide nanofluid. An increasing trend in viscosity with nanoparticle concentrations was reported. Dezfulizadeh et al. [47] synthesized the aqueous Cu-SiO2-MWCNT nanofluid and measured its viscosity. The Newtonian behaviour of the nanofluid and its higher viscosity compared to nanofluids containing one or two nanoparticles were reported. Adun et al. [48] experimentally inspected the viscosity of aqueous Al2O3-ZnO-Fe3O4 nanofluid. The mixture ratios of 1:1:1, 1:2:1, and 1:1:2 were considered for nanoparticles, and the concentration of nanoparticles was in the range of 0–1.25%. The measured viscosity of the nanofluid was in the range of 0.001–0.021 Pa.s.

When a designer wants to replace a working fluid in a heat transfer system with a nanofluid, the first question that arises is which of the many options available is the most appropriate for that particular application. The synthesis of a nanofluid and the measurement of its thermophysical properties are both time-consuming and costly. Therefore, the designer cannot synthesize several different nanofluids and study their cooling performance. To solve this problem, various researchers have used techniques such as neural networks to develop predictive models for the thermophysical properties of nanofluids based on laboratory data [49-51]. These relationships help designers use numerical simulations to first evaluate the performance of different nanofluids in the intended application and finally synthesize the nanofluid that has the best performance and test its performance experimentally.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of carbonaceous materials that have recently grabbed recognition as a desirable material due to their optical and fluorescent properties, low cost, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, photo-stability, favorable charge transfers with enhanced electronic conductivity, comparable easy-synthesis protocols and high thermal conductivity [52,53]. They are used in biosensors, drug delivery, photo-catalysis, energy applications (supercapacitors, photovoltaics), heat transfer systems, and water and wastewater treatment [54,55]. Nanofluids containing CDs have recently been considered by researchers, and so far, the viscosity of water-Cu/CD [56], water-SiO2/CD [57], water-Ag/CD [58], water/ethylene glycol-CD [55,59], and car radiator coolant-Fe3O4/CD and radiator coolant-CuO/CD [60] have been measured. A survey of articles published on the nanofluids containing CDs reveals that no studies have been published on the viscosity of PEG-CD nanofluids. Therefore, this issue is investigated in the present study. The PEG200-based nanofluid containing CD nanoparticles was prepared, and its rheological behavior was investigated at different nanoparticle concentrations and temperatures. Also, soft computing modeling based on the
φ
, temperature, and the shear rate was addressed to the precise estimation of viscosity of PEG-CD nanofluids using a new predictive model, namely, UKF-ANN approach. For examining the outcomes of UKF-ANN, the MLP and RSM approaches were implemented, and various validation analyses were performed.

Based on previously reported methods [54,55], preparation of the highly stable carbonaceous nanofluid was followed by a fast and one-pot synthesis method. Briefly, in a Pyrex beaker, 70 ml polyethylene glycol 200 was added and heated with a microwave oven (Moulinex, Serie: MW02-A, China), operating at 700 W for 10 min. During microwave irradiation, polar groups of polyethylene glycol absorb the microwaves. The result of this kind of interaction appears in the temperature increment of solution (up to ∼ 300 °C after 10 min microwave illumination, which was monitored by an infrared thermal imaging camera (Hti HT-18) (Fig. 1 )), leading to the carbonization of polyethylene glycol. So, the color of the sample gradually turns dark. This dark color is the sign of carbon particles formation. As the obtained dark fluid is prepared at high-temperature conditions, it is very stable and can endure high-temperature environments.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM) was carried out on Philips EM208s for investigation of the morphology of the dispersed particles in the obtained nanofluids and also the particle size distribution of them. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Zetasizer Ver. 7.11, serial number: MAL1153055) technique was used for measurement of the particle size distribution and the average hydrodynamic diameter. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Perkin-Elmer Version 10.02.00) was used for functional group characterization of the prepared carbon nanofluid.

Based on the TEM image of the dispersed carbon particles in the prepared nanofluid (Fig. 2 a), most of the synthesized carbon particles have spherical morphology with 2 to25 nm diameters. From the obtained particle size distribution histogram, most of the carbonaceous nanoparticles have a diameter < 10 nm, and the average diameter of the carbon particles is about 5.5 nm, indicating the fact that the carbon dots (CDs) were produced under microwave illumination of polyethylene glycol.

Fig. 3 illustrates the hydrodynamic size distribution of the CDs dispersed in the diluted nanofluid based on DLS analysis. The average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs dispersed in PEG is ∼ 80 nm which is longer than the average diameter of the CDs obtained by TEM (∼5.5 nm). This difference arises from the fact that the average hydrodynamic diameter corresponds to the diameter of the core and its protective shell. Therefore, it should be higher than the average diameter. As the thickness of the protective shell is large, the dispersion stability of CDs in polyethylene glycol is high.

The FT-IR spectra of the prepared CDs nanofluid and polyethylene glycol are shown in Fig. 4 . The spectra of both mentioned samples are nearly similar to each other, which is because of the low concentration of the CDs in the base fluid. However, the FT-IR spectrum of the prepared CDs nanofluid shows a slight broadening compared to that of pure polyethylene glycol, which is due to changing the chemical environment when a specific element may be bonding with other chemical groups [61]. Broadening of the peaks centered at 3353, 2877, and 1093 cm − 1, which belong to the O-H, C-H, and C-O stretching, respectively, reveal the interaction of polyethylene glycol functional groups with carbon nanoparticles.

A Paar Physica MCR300 parallel disc rheometer is used to study the rheological behavior of the prepared nanofluid samples. This device is very accurate, and its error is only
±
0.002 × 106 kg/m.s. This device is equipped with a circulating bath and a controlled Peltier unit, with the help of which the viscosity of the sample can be measured at different temperatures.

It is well known that artificial neural networks (ANNs) can accurately show how data changes in non-linear and multivariate systems [62]. These numerical models can learn and recognize the non-linear functions that govern different types of data by copying the biological neural network of the human brain. Many simple parts make up these networks, like neurons, that do computing work [63]. They have been put together so that one layer is the input, another layer is hidden, and another layer is the output. In addition, artificial neural networks have interconnections called weights that change the signal sent between neurons in different layers by increasing or decreasing their effectiveness [64]. They move data from one layer to another in a not obvious way. Hidden layer: Each neuron in the hidden layer receives a weighted input from a neuron in the previous layer, as well as a bias. The number of nodes (neurons) in the input and output variables is determined by the input and output layers. Each MLP node can implement two functions: summation and activation. In the following equation, the summation function is used to sum the product of inputs, weights, and bias [65]:
(1)
S j
=
∑
i = 1
N
w
ij
I i
+
β j

where
n
represents the number of inputs,
I i
represents the input variable,
β j
is a bias term, and
w
ij
represents the connection weight. Second, using the output of Eq. (1), initiate an activation function. In the MLP, various types of activation functions can be used. In previous works, the S-shaped curved sigmoid function was the most commonly used type:
(2)
f i
x
=
1
1 +
e
-
S j

As a result, the neuron j final output can be calculated using the following equation [65]:
(3)
y i
=
f i
∑
i = 1
n
w
ij
I i
+
β j

Following the design of the ANN structure, the learning step is carried out to fine-tune and update the network weights. These weights are rationalized in order to calculate the results and minimize the results' error [66].

The use of Kalman filters to solve optimal estimation problems has been the subject of much research in the last few decades. However, continued research has established that the original Kalman filters are frequently insufficient for state estimation, particularly non-linear systems [67]. The extended Kalman filter (EKF) is a variant of the Kalman filter that is used to deal with non-linear systems. The EKF accomplishes this modification by linearizing all non-linear models, allowing for the application of the traditional Kalman filter. Regrettably, the EKF has two significant potential disadvantages. 1) The derivation of Jacobian matrices, the linear approximators to non-linear functions, can be complicated, posing implementation challenges. 2) If the time step intervals are not sufficiently small, this linearization can result in filter instability [68]. To overcome these limitations, Julier and Uhlmann [69] proposed the unscented Kalman filter (UKF), which was later developed further by Wan et al. [70].

Kalman filters and unscented transformation were used together to make it easier to figure out the state variables of large, non-linear dynamic systems [69]. UKF is based on the unscented transformation (UT) theory. It can better handle nonlinearities in one-step-ahead prediction because of this. For a non-linear, a statistical distribution is spread through the system. This gives better estimates of the actual state and the covariance matrices that will come later. This paper does not use the standard gradient or backpropagation algorithm to change the MLP weights and other settings. Instead, we use a UKF to make the MLP more accurate and faster to adapt to chaotic changes in the inputs.

Consider the following non-linear discrete models that can be used to describe the behavior of an MLP:
(4)
w

=

μ k
(5)

In the above equations,
y =
L + λ
, where
L
is the state's dimension,
λ
is the composite scaling parameter,
W i
is the network's weights,
R
e
is the measurement noise covariance and
R
r
is the process noise covariance. Fig. 5 depicts the structure of a single-layer neural network trained with an unscented Kalman filter.

RSM has established itself as a highly effective method for explaining and demonstrating the mathematical link between input factors and output responses [71]. One of the advantages of RSM is that it is more beneficial when the influence of several factors on one or more responses must be investigated to minimize the number of experiments required. Additionally, setting a series of criteria for each independent variable may be used to optimize some of the responses. Furthermore, sufficient interpretation of experiments for the non-linear surface of the results experiments is an important aspect of RSM analysis [72].

The RSM models can form a linear mathematical relationship with unknown variables and responses. Even though most data contain curvature, linear models between input variables and responses are not appropriate in most cases. The second-order function in Eq. (1) indicates that a polynomial model with greater degrees is most suited in this case [71,73]:
(18)
Y =
B 0
+
∑
i = 1
N
B i
X i
+
∑
i = 1
N
B
ii
X
ii
2
+ ∑
B
ij
X i
X j
+ E

Where Y is the response variable,
X
denotes the input variable, B0 is the intercept term,
B i
is the coefficient of the linear term,
E
is error term,
B
ij
is the coefficient of the interaction between variables, and
B
ii
is the coefficient of the quadratic term.

In order to evaluate the performance of the various models, the correlation coefficient (R), root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), Willmott's agreement Index (IA), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and coefficient of uncertainty with a 95 percent confidence level (
U
95 %
) were used to evaluate the models [74].

where

are the predicted and actual nanofluid density values, respectively.

¯
is the mean of the predicted results.

¯

is the average of the observed values of wave energy. N represents the total number of samples. The standard deviation of estimating error is denoted by
SD
e
. For an ideal model the values of metrics are R = 1, RMSE = 0, MAE = 0, MAPE = 0, and U95% = 0.

This section consists of two sub-sections, the first of which is devoted to providing the rheological behaviour of the PEG-CD measured for various values of shear rate (1–400 s−1), temperature (20–60 °C), and volume concentration of nanoparticles (0–0.07%) and the second devoted to obtaining an accurate model for estimating the nanofluid viscosity based on critical parameters (
φ
, temperature, and shear rate) utilizing three data-driven models including UKF-ANN, classical ANN, and RSM approaches.

Figs. 6-9
disclose the effect of temperature on the rheological behaviour of PEG-CD nanofluid for
φ
= 0%, 1%, 3%, and 7%, respectively. Fig. 6 indicates that the change in shear rate has no effect on the viscosity of the pure PEG, which means that the PEG is a Newtonian fluid. This observation is consistent with previous studies [75,76]. Examination of the results presented in Figs. 7 to 9 reveals that the addition of CDs to the PEG leads to its non-Newtonian behavior in a way that the viscosity of the nanofluid decreases with increasing shear rate. The rate of decrease in viscosity with increasing shear rate is greater at higher temperatures, while the percentage of decrease does not change much with changing nanoparticle concentrations and is in the range of 9.16–44.24% for all nanofluids. The non-Newtonian behavior of the nanofluid can be explained by the fact that initially, there are clumps of nanoparticles in the nanofluid, but after the shear force is applied, they disappear, and the nanofluid moves more easily. In addition, it is observed that with increasing temperature, the viscosity of the nanofluid decreases, which is due to the increased Brownian motion of the nanoparticles. The rate of decrease in viscosity with increasing temperature for nanofluids with
φ
of 0%, 1%, 3% and 7% is 78.21%, 63.50–73.21%, 65.76–75.21% and 67.26–77.21%, respectively.

In this research, the target is considered three input features: volume fraction, temperature, shear rate, and viscosity. Table 1 lists the descriptive statistics of the variables used in the modeling. The skewness and kurtosis values demonstrate that all the variables are in the allowable domain of (-2,2). Also, for identifying the most significant input features, the correlogram of inputs and target is illustrated in Fig. 10 . the Pearson correlation values ascertained that T, regarding the highest correlation value (Pc = -0.87) has the most significant effect in the prediction of viscosity followed by volume fraction (Pc = 0.34), and shear rate (PC = -0.1), respectively. It should be mentioned that the negative value of Pearson correlation in a feature shows its inverse effect on the target.

To model the viscosity of PEG-carbon dot nanofluid, a novel machine learning approach called Unscented Kalman Filter integrated with the artificial neural network (UKF-ANN) along with MLP and RSM models were adopted, which all of them were developed in the Matlab 2020a environment. Here, three input variables, including the
φ
, temperature (T), and shear rate (
γ
), were considered, whereas the viscosity (
μ
) was the modeling target. In this study, the K-fold cross-validation (with 10 Folds) approach was employed to avoid overfitting in the models. By this technique, all the datasets participate in both training and testing phases. Here, to achieve accurate results, it is necessary to optimize the main parameters of the UKF-ANN and MLP models, including the neurons number, number of the epoch, and network architecture. For this purpose, the grid search strategy was used in the UKF-ANN and MLP. The optimal neuron number and epoch value in the UKF-ANN were obtained equal to 10 and 100, whereas, in the MLP, the best architecture was gained as 3–6-1. Fig. 11 demonstrates the workflow of predicting the viscosity of PEG-carbon dot nanofluid based on three efficient data-driven models.

As a quadratic approximation method, RSM has always been widely considered in analyzing problems related to the thermophysical properties of nanofluids. In many cases, it leads to promising results. One of the advantages of this method is to provide a second-order relationship for estimating the target parameter based on input features. Table 2 tabulated the outcomes of the quadratic analysis of the RSM technique, and the final relationship can be expressed as follows:
(24)
μ = 140.04 + 12.454 φ - 4.08 T - 0.027 γ - 0.087 φ T - 0.003 φ γ - 0.697
φ
2
+ 0.035
T
2
+ 6.27 ×
10
- 5
γ
2

In the continuation of this section, the results of the viscosity simulation for the PEG-carbon dot nanofluid have been assessed based on five statistical criteria, various graphical tools, and a comprehensive diagnostic analysis. Table 3 lists the performance metric related to the three predictive models. According to testing results in Table 3, the UKF-ANN owing to best accuracy in terms of (R = 0.9996, RMSE = 0.8194 kg/m.s, MAPE = 1.5936, and U95%=2.2390) outperformed the MLP (R = 0.9986, RMSE = 1.3798 kg/m.s, MAPE = 2.9907, and U95%=3.8346), and RSM (R = 0.9929, RMSE = 3.0739 kg/m.s, MAPE = 5.8474, and U95%=8.5411), respectively. On the other hand, the MLP method was ranked as the second-best model regarding the better statistical criteria, and RSM was introduced as the last precise model.

Fig. 12 demonstrates the scatter diagram to compare the predictive performance between experimental and measurement viscosity values of the PEG-carbon dot nanofluid. As can be seen, the viscosity values estimated using the UKF-ANN method are much more consistent with the actual viscosity values than the other two models, and the scatter of points around the 45° line is the lowest value among the three provided models. Another efficient validation approach for the data-driven methods is to compare the probability distribution function (PDF) of the predicted and actual viscosity using a smooth kernel. Fig. 13 illustrates the rain cloud PDF plot of experimental and estimated viscosity of PEG-carbon dot nanofluid via developed data-driven models. According to the quartile criteria, UKF-ANN (Q25%=28.13, Q75%=63.6, and IQR = 35.47), due to the highest distribution similarity with the experimental values of viscosity (Q25%=28.4, Q75%=63.8, and IQR = 35.4) outperformed the MLP (Q25%=27.54, Q75%=68.38, and IQR = 36.13), and RSM (Q25%=27.21, Q75%=68.38, and IQR = 41.17), respectively.

Finally, a comprehensive diagnostic analysis was performed to evaluate the reliability of the outcomes shown in Fig. 14 . the left side of Fig. 14 depicted the 3D plot of relative deviation (RD) of each predictive model, and the right side of one showed the violin plot of RD for all the models in the testing phase. According to the violin plot of RD (%), the UKF-ANN model owing to the least range of RD (11.48%), yielded the most reliable outcome in comparison with the MLP (19.23%) model and RSM (56.45%). Besides, the left side of Fig. 14 exhibits that most RD values in all the models occurred in
40 ⩽ T ⩽ 50
and
φ ⩽ 2
.

The purpose of this laboratory study was to investigate the rheological behavior of PEG-CD nanofluids at different values of temperature and
φ
. For this purpose, nanofluids containing nanoparticles with
φ
of 1%, 3%, and 7% were prepared, and after determining the characteristics, their viscosity was measured at temperatures of 20, 30, 40, and 50 and 60 °C. It was found that the base fluid had Newtonian behavior while the nanofluid exhibited non-Newtonian behavior. In addition, it was observed that the non-Newtonian behavior of the nanofluid intensifies with increasing temperature but is independent of
φ
. Increasing the shear rate resulted in a 9.16–44.24% reduction in viscosity at different temperatures. Also, it was observed that the viscosity decreases and increases with increasing temperature and
φ
, respectively. In the second part of this research, a soft computing assessment was adopted to accurate estimation of the viscosity of PEG-carbon dot nanofluid using an optimized version of ANN (i.e., UKF-ANN) and two traditional approaches, namely MLP and RSM models. UKF technique as a suboptimalfiltration algorithm can be efficient for coupling with the machine learning approach to solve non-linear problems. In this regard, three features comprised of
φ
, temperature, and shear rate were considered as the input parameters. The outcomes indicated that the accuracy of UKF-ANN regarding (R = 0.9996, RMSE = 0.8194 kg/m.s, and U95%=2.2390) was superior to the MLP (R = 0.9986, RMSE = 1.3798 kg/m.s, and U95%=3.8346) and RSM (R = 0.9929, RMSE = 3.0739 kg/m.s, and U95%=8.5411), respectively. Besides, UKF-ANN, according to the least value of RD (11.48%), resulted in the most reliable outcomes than MLP (19.23%) and RSM (56.45%)) approaches. It is worth mentioning that researchers can consider the RSM method with appropriate accuracy due to providing a simple relationship to estimate the rheological property of the PEG-carbon dot nanofluid. The correlation analysis proved that the temperature was identified as the most influential feature in estimating the understudy nonfluid.
In the future study, the cooling performance of the prepared nanofluid in a concentrated photovoltaic/thermal system will be investigated experimentally.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
